text: CHAPELLE - D'ANGILLON (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Cher, arrond. of Sancerre. The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,707; in 1841, 5,917. The town, which is a small one, lies on the Sauldre, on the road from Paris to Bourges, 22 m. W of Sancerre. Pop. 739. It has an annual fair for the sale of horses, cattle, sheep, pigs, goats, asses, &c.
CHAPELLE-D'ARMENTIE'RES (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nord, cant. of Armentières. Pop. 1,889. It contains manufactories of sugar from beet-root.
CHAPELLE-DU-BARD (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isere, cant. of Allevard, 6 m. from Grenoble. Pop. 1,332.
CHAPELLE - BANHAC (LA). See CAPELLEBANHAC.
CHAPELLE-BASSE-MER (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Loroux, 13 m. ENE of Nantes. Pop. in 1841, 4,336.
CHAPELLE-BICHE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Flers, 9 m. N of Domfront. Pop. 1,019. It contains manufactories of ticking and nails, and has a trade in timber.
CHAPELLE-BLANCHE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Côtes-du-Nord, cant. of Saint-Jouan-de-l'Isle. Pop. 457.
CHAPELLE-AUX-BOIS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Vosges, cant. of Xertigny, 13 m. SSW of Epinal. Pop. 2,549.
CHAPELLE - DU - BOIS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of La Ferté- Bernard. Pop. 1,105.
CHAPELLE-BOUEXIC (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Maure. Pop. 991.
CHAPELLE-CHAUSSE'E (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Becherel, 12 m. NNE of Montfort. Pop. 1,204.
CHAPELLE-SUR-CRE'CY (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of and near Crécy, 9 m. S of Meaux, on the Grand Morin. Pop. 1,249.
CHAPELLE-SOUS-DUN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of La Clayette. Pop. 490. It contains coal-mines, the produce of which amounted in 1839 to 94,371 quintals.
CHAPELLE-ERBREE (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 6 m. E of Vitré. Pop. 688.

CHAPELLE-SUR-ERDRE (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, arrond. of Nantes. The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,951; in 1841, 10,578. The village lies near the l. bank of the Erdre, 6 m. N of Nantes. Pop. in 1846, 2,420. In the environs is a mineral spring.

CHAPELLE-GAULHIER (LA), or LA CHAPELLE-THIBOUST, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Marne, cant. of Mormant, 13 m. E of Melun. Pop. 943. This was formerly a town of some importance. A fair is held once a-year.
CHAPELLE-GLAIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of St.-Juliende-Vouvantes, 13 m. SE of Chateaubriant. Pop. 1,128.

CHAPELLE-DE-GUINCHAY (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Saoneet-Loire. The cant. comprises 12 com. Pop. in CHA 1831, 9,902; in 1841, 10,015. The village is near the r. bank of the Saone, 8 m. S of Macon. Pop. 1,906. The environs afford good wine.
CHAPELLE - HEULIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Vallet, 12 m. ESE of Nantes. Pop. 1,357. It affords good wine.
CHAPELLE-JANSON (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. and 6 m. E of Fougères. Pop. 2,013.
CHAPELLE-EN-JUGER (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Manche, cant. of Marigny. Pop. 1,442.
CHAPELLE - LANNAY (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. and 2 m. NW of Sayenay. Pop. 1,423.

CHAPELLE-SUR-LOIRE (LA), or LA-CHAPELLE-BLANCHE-SUR-LOIRE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Indre-et-Loire, cant. of Bourgueil, on the r. bank of the Loire, 6 m. N of Chinon. Pop. in 1841, 3,575. It has 4 annual fairs.
CHAPELLE-DES-MARAIS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Herbignac, 18 m. NW of Savenay. Pop. 1,855.
CHAPELLE-MOCHE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Juvignysous-Andaine, 12 m. SE of Domfront. Pop. 2,816. It has a considerable trade in lint.
CHAPELLE-MONTBRANDEIX (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint-Mathieu. Pop. 869. It contains some wire-mills.
CHAPELLE-MONTLIGEON (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Orne, cant. of Mortagne. Pop. 1,014.
CHAPELLE-MONTREUIL (LA). See Mon- TREUIL-BONNIN.
CHAPELLE-SUR-OREUSE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Sergines, 9 m. NE of Sens, on the Oreuse. Pop. 486.
CHAPELLE-PALLUAU (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vendee, cant. of Palluau, 27 m. NNE of Les Sables. Pop. 1,105.
CHAPELLE-AUX-POTS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. of Coudray- Saint-Germer, 9 m. from Beauvais. Pop. in 1841, 695. It has extensive manufactories of stoneware.

CHAPELLE-LA-REINE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et- Marne, arrond. of Fontainebleau. The cant. comprises 20 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,171; in 1841, 9,113. The town is 12 m. SW of Fontainebleau, on an eminence. Pop. 867.
CHAPELLE-AU-RIBOULE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and 9 m. E of Mayenne, cant. of Le Horps. Pop. 1,137.
CHAPELLE - SOUS - ROUGEMONT (LA), or
KAPELLEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haut-Rhin, cant. of Fontaine. Pop. 714.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-ANDRE' (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. of Varcy, 12 m. from Clamecy. Pop. 1,123. It contains a blast-furnace, and numerous iron-forges.

CHAPELLE-SAINT-DENIS (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Seine, cant. and 4 m. S of Saint-Denis, and forming a suburb of Paris. Pop. in 1831, 2,727; in 1841, 8,724. It contains extensive manufactories of perfumery, wax-cloth, oil, silk, silkhats, ropes, vinegar, &c.; and distilleries of brandy. It has important cattle and pig-markets, and is the entrepot for cheese for the supply of the capital. It is noted as the birth-place of the poet Chapelle.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-FLORENT (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. of Saint-Florent-le-Viel. Pop. 1,156.

CHAPELLE-SAINT-LAURENT (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Deux- Sevres, cant. of Moncontat, 15 m. NW of Parthenay. Pop. 1,353. It contains a tile-work; and has an annual fair, the trade of which consists in cattle, articles of local manufacture, flannel, stuffs, &c.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-MARTIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Mer, 12 m. NNE of Blois. Pop. 1,052.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-MESMIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. and on the r. bank of the Loiret, cant. and 3 m. WSW of Orleans. Pop. 1,282. It contains some paper-mills, and produces good wine.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-PIERRE (LA). See GRANDES-CHAPELLES.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-QUILLAIN (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Saone, cant. of Gy, 12 m. from Gray. Pop. 571. It contains some iron-mines.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-REMY (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Sarthe, cant. of Tuffe. Pop. 1,131.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-SAUVEUR (LA), or LA
CHAPELLE-MONTRELAIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Varades, 12 m. NE of Ancenis. Pop. 1,308.
CHAPELLE-SAINT-SAUVEUR (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Pierre, 16 m. N of Louhans. Pop. 1,814.
CHAPELLE - EN - SERVAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Oise, cant. and 6 m. from Senlis. Pop. 544.
CHAPELLE-TAILLEFERT (LA), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of the Creuse, cant. and 7 m. S of Gueret, on the l. bank of the Gartempe. Pop. 800.
CHAPELLE - THE CLE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saône-et-Loire, cant. of Montpont. Pop. 1,292.
CHAPELLE-VENDOMAISE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Herbault. Pop. 424.

CHAPELLE-EN-VERCORS (LA), a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Drôme, arrond. of Die.—The cant. comprises 5 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,111; in 1841, 5,067.—The village lies in the valley of Vercors, on the Vernaison, 16 m. N of Die. Pop. 1,309. It has some trade in timber, charcoal, and cattle; and has 4 annual fairs. Iron in small quantities, and alabaster, are found in the environs.

CHAPELLE - VOLANT (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Jura, cant. of Bletterans, on the Darge, 13 m. NW of Lons-le-Saunier. Pop. 1,907. CHAPELLES (GRANDES), OF LA CHAPELLESAINT-PIERRE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Aube, cant. of Méry-sur-Seine, 9 m. from Arcissur-Aube. Pop. 758. It contains manufactories of serge, and of cotton hosiery.

CHAPINERIA, a town of Spain, in New Castile, in the prov. and 30 m. WSW of Madrid, partido of Navalcarnero, between the Alberche and Perales, at the foot of a hill. Pop. 875. It contains a customhouse, and has a considerable trade in cattle.
CHAPIO-D'UVAS. See Engenho-do-Mato.
CHAPLIN, a township of Windham co., in the state of Connecticut, U. S., 37 m. NE of Hartford. Pop. 794. It is watered by Nantchang river, a branch of Shetucket river, and has a good soil.

CHAPMAN, a township of Union co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U. S., 42 m. NW of Harrisburg. CHA CHAPELLE-SAINT-HIPPOLYTE (LA). See | is watered by Mahantango creek, which affords good water-power. Pop. in 1840, 1,297 -Also a township of Clinton co., in the same state. Pop. 622.
CHAPNIERS. See CHANIERS.

CHA-PO, or Cha-PU, a district, town, and harbour of China, in the N extremity of Che-kyang prov., in N lat. 30° 33', E long. 120° 40', 75 m. from Chusan. The town is built in the form of a square; and is nearly 5 m. in circuit. It is situated near the W extremity of a small promontory stretching E and W between 4 and 5 m. The suburbs run along the edge of the beach. The actual walled town stands about a 1 m. in the rear; and the Tartar town or quarter is separated from the other part of the city by a wall. It is intersected by numerous canals, by which it is connected with the city of Hang-cho. The surrounding country "is perhaps one of the richest and most beautifully cultivated spots in the world" [W. H. Hall]. As far as the eye can range, all is a series of hamlets and villages, interspersed with towering pagodas, romantic mausoleums, and numerous temples, and intersected by canals and causeways. Wherever a barren spot precludes cultivation, it is used as a cemetery. C. is the only place from which the Chinese commerce with Japan -an imperial monopoly-is carried on. The staple import from Japan is copper; the exports from C. are manufactures and raw produce, and British goods. The harbour is tolerable, but with a great rise and fall of tide, so that the junks lie high and dry at low water. The sea is everywhere in this quarter receding from the land, so that the flats formed along the shore, which are dry at low water, constitute a barrier to the whole coast, and are gradually becoming arable soil. [Gutzlaff.]—C. was taken by the British forces in May 1842.
CHAPONNAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Saint-Symphorien-d'Ozon, 9 m. N of Vienne. Pop. 1,163.

CHAPONOST, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Saint-Genis-Laval, 6 m. SW of Lyons. Pop. 1,588. It possesses manufactories of nets and carding-combs; and pure barytes is wrought here.

CHAPPELL ISLANDS, a low and level group of three islets, on the NE coast of Australia. There is a lighthouse on the outer isle of the group, called Goose island, in N lat. 40° 18′ 41″, W long. 147° 48′ 50". It is elevated 108 ft. above high water, and is seen at a distance of 30 m. in clear weather.
CHAPPES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Ennezat, on the r. bank of the Allier. Pop. 776. Boat-building forms the chief branch of local industry.
CHAPRUNG, a town and fort of Tibet, in Tsang or Ulterior Tibet, on the l. bank of the Sutluj, 50 m. NE of Gangoutri. It is the residence of a Chinese zumpung or governor.

CHAPTES (SAINT), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Gard, arrond. of Uzes. The cant. comprises 16 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,334; in 1841, 8,568.—The town is near the l. bank of the Gardon, 9 m. SE of Uzès. Pop. 758. It contains a Calvinist consistorial church.

CHAPUS, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Charente-Inferieure, cant., com., and 5 m. NW of Marennes, on the Courreaux d'Oléron, opposite the island of that name, and 27 m. S of La Rochelle. It is defended by a fort, and possesses a small port.
CHAQUI, a town of Bolivia, in the intendancy and 25 m. ENE of Potosi, prov. of Charcas, on the r. bank of the Paspaya.
CHARA, or ALAK-Nor, a lake of Mongolia, in It presents a hilly surface, with a gravelly soil; and | Inner Mongolia, to the NE of the Alak mountains. *
CHARACATA, a town of Bolivia, 10 m. SE of 1 Arequipa, on the r. bank of the Quilca.
CHARAGOLSK, a town and fort of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 220 m. SE of Irkoutsk, near the Chinese frontier.
CHARANCE, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Hautes-Alpes, cant. and 3 m. WNW of Gap. It contains extensive quarries of marble.
CHARAPOTO, a town of New Granada, in the prov. and 110 m. NNW of Guayaquil, on a river which flows into a bay of the same name.
CHARATON. See CHARITON.
CHARBAGH, a town of Afghanistan, in the district of Ghoraut, 30 m. N of Herat.

CHARBA'R, a town of Beluchistan, on the E shore of the Persian gulf, on a bay of the same name. It is surrounded by a mud wall, on which a few old guns are mounted. The streets are narrow and dirty, and the houses are chiefly built of mud. Pop. about 1,500. The district belongs to the imam of Muskat, who appoints a sheikh to govern it. In the vicinity are several date-groves, with a few fields producing corn and vegetables; but the whole coast partakes of the arid sterile character which prevails from the Indus to Bagdad. To the N of the town are extensive ruins, supposed to indicate the site of Tiz, mentioned by Idrisi in the 12th cent.-Kempthorne.
CHARBASHI, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorassan, 40 m. SSE of Nain, on the road to Yezd. It was visited by Christie in 1810.
CHARBONNIER, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Saint-Germain-Lembron, 12 m. S of Issoire. Pop. 180. Coal is wrought in the environs.

CHARBONNIE'RE (LA), a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Nievre, cant. and immediately adjacent to Decise, com. of Saint-Leger-des-Vignes, on the r. bank of the Loire. Pop. 290. It contains bottleworks, blast-furnaces, and iron-forges; and has a small port. In the environs are extensive coal-mines.
CHARBONNIE'RES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Vaugneray, 4 m. W of Lyons. Pop. 346. It contains a castle; and is celebrated for its fine park, and its mineral springs.
CHARBONNIE'RES-LES-VARENNES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Manzat. Pop. 1,318.
CHARBONNIE'RES-LES-VIEILLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, cant. of Manzat, 12 m. NW of Riom. Pop. 2,159.
CHARBUY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, cant. of Auxerre. Pop. 1,270.
CHARCAS. See CHUQUISACA.
CHARCAS (SANTA-MARIA-DE-LAS), a town of Mexico, in the prov. and 60 m. N of San-Luis- Potosi, on the r. bank of the Santander.

CHARD, a parish and borough in Somersetshire, at the S extremity of the county. Area of p. 5,140 acres. Pop. 5,788: of whom 2,877 were in the borough of C.; 1,331 in the tything of Old C.; 471 in the tything of South C.; 517 in the tything of Cum-C.; 209 in the tything of Foxton; and 383 in that of Tatworth. There are several woollen-mills in the p.; and the manufacture of lace has recently been introduced.

CHARDAK, or HAJI-GOL [i. e. 'Pilgrim lake'], a large salt lake of Anatolia, in 37° 50' N lat., and 30° E long., supposed to be the ancient Ascania. It lies about 12 m. SW of Dineir, and to the NW of the Buldur-göl.
CHARDAR, a pass leading over the Hindu-kush from Afghanistan into Kunduz, in N lat. 34° 55'.

CHARDEH, or CHAHAR DEH, [i. e. 'the Four villages, a fort between Candahar and Ghuzni, in Af- the soil. Cattle are extensively reared; and the pro- CHA ghanistan, 30 m. SW of Ghuzni.—Also a v. of Afghanistan, on the r. bank of the Cabul river, in the valley of Jellalabad, 14 m. E of Ali Bogan, at an alt. of 1,822 ft. above sea-level. The surrounding territory is a barren stony desert, where, in the months of April and May, the simoom wind prevails.
CHARDIK, a town of Khorassan, 15 m. NW of Tabbus, on the E skirts of the great Salt desert.
CHARDOGNE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Meuse, cant. of Vavincourt. Pop. 587.
CHARDON, a town in Geauga co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 170 m. NE of Columbus, 600 ft. above the level of Lake Erie, and 14 m. S of Fairport, on that lake. Pop. 1,064.
CHARDSTOCK, a parish in Dorsetshire, 3½ m. SSW of Chard. Area 5,540 acres. Pop. 1,405.
CHARENCEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Moselle, cant. of Longuyon, on the r. bank of the Chiers. Pop. 1,072.
CHARENSAT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dome, cant. of Saint-Gervais. Pop. 1,676.

CHARENTE, a river of France, which rises at Cheronnat, to the SW of Rochechouart, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne; flows NW to Civray, where it turns S, and flowing in that direction through the dep. of Charente, passes Ruffec, Mansle, and Angouleme; at the latter town it bends round to the W; after which it passes Chateauneuf and Cognac; enters the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, in which it passes Saintes, Taillebourg, Tonnay-Charente, Rochefort, and Soubise; and flows into the Atlantic opposite the isle of Oleron, after a course of about 200 m.—Its principal affluents on the l. are the Argentor, the Son, the Bonnieux, the Touvre, the Né, and the Seugne; on the r. the Lemps, the Anteine, and the Boutonne. It is navigable by rafts from Civray to Montignac; and from the latter point to the sea by small vessels, with the aid of some artificial works between Montignac and Jarnac. From Rochefort downwards it is navigable by ships of war.—Wood, brandy, wine, corn, flax, and gypsum, are the principal articles of transit on this river.—This river, the Carantonus of the Romans, gives name to two departments in the modern division of France.

CHARENTE, a department in the W of France, comprising the ancient Angoumois, and some parts of Lemousin, Poitou, Saintonge, and Perigord. It takes it name from the river Charente, which has the greater part of its upper course within this dep. It lies between the parallels of 45° 12' and 46° 8' N; and is bounded on the N by the dep. of Deux-Sevres and Vienne; on the E by Haute-Vienne; on the S by Dordogne; and on the W by Charente-Inferieure. Its greatest length from NE to SW is 78 m.; its greatest breadth from E to W, 54 m. Its area is <math>594,740</math> hectares <math>= 1,469,662</math> acres.—The surface is generally hilly. The most elevated points are in the NE, where the hills may be regarded as a prolongation of the mountains of Limousin. The greater part of the surface belongs to the basin of the Charente; the NE section, which is watered by the Vienne, to the basin of the Loire; and the S, which is watered by the Dronne, to the basin of the Garonne. Numerous lakes occur in the arrond, of Confolens, in the valley of the Vienne. — The climate is temperate. The prevailing winds are the W and SW.—About onehalf of the surface is arable; nearly a sixth is occupied with vineyards; and about one-seventh with woods and forests. In the arrond. of Confolens and of Barbezieux, extensive landes or wastes occur, of which only a small portion is capable of cultivation: nearly 70,000 hectares of the surface are thus occupied with irreclaimable wastes.—Agricultural industry in this dep. is considerably checked by the nature of Iron, lead, gypsum, and chalk, are wrought; and the produce of the forests in wood and charcoal is considerable. Truffles form an important article of produce, amounting in annual value to 300,000 francs. -The principal articles of manufacture are ironware, paper, leather, ropes, nut and colza oil, and earthenware. Angouleme is the grand commercial entrepot of the dep.—The administrative division of this dep. consists of 5 arrondissements, 29 cantons, and 454 communes. The pop. in 1801 was 299,029; in 1831, 362,531. The pop. of the 5 arrondissements was as follows in 1841 and in 1846: The average pop. per square kilometre is 62.83; that for all France being 67.088. The number of electors under the late regime was 3,463; number of deputies, 5. The dep. forms the diocese of the bishop of Angouleme. In 1839 it had 3 communal colleges: viz., those of Angouleme, Confolens, and Larochefoucauld; with 269 pupils; 405 primary boys' schools with 14,776 scholars; and 65 girls' schools with 3,330 scholars.—The annual territorial revenue is estimated at 17,906,000 francs.
CHARENTE. See TONNAY-CHARENTE.

CHARENTE-INFE'RIEURE, a department of France, formed of the ancient provinces of Angoumois and Poitou. It lies between the parallels of 45° 5' and 46° 22' N; and is bounded on the N by La Vendée; NE by Deux-Sevres; on the E by Charente; on the S by Gironde and the estuary of the Garonne; and on the W by the Atlantic ocean. Its area, comprising the isles of Ré, Oleron, Madame, and Aix, amounts to 675,375 hectares. Of this area 329,000 hect. are arable; 112,000 are under vines; 72,000 in timber; and 14,000 in heaths and waste lands. The general aspect of the country is flat and marshy. The Charente traverses the dep. from E to W, in the lower part of its course; and receives the Boutonne on the r. and the Seugne on the l., in this dep. The other rivers are the Sevre-Niortaise, the Seudre, and the Gironde. The coast-line extends to about 65 m., and presents several good harbours and roads; and internal navigation is facilitated by the canals of Niort and Brouage, and the Charente and the Boutonne.—The soil is generally fertile. About onethird of the surface is devoted to the cultivation of the vine, the annual produce of which is estimated at £1,000,000. In 1839, the salt marshes in this dep. were estimated to present a total area of 10,579 hectares, and to produce 1,160,042 quintals of salt. The principal articles of export are brandy, salt, corn, wine, vinegar, wood, cattle, oysters, sardines, earthenware, and refined sugar. The total entries inward and outward of the 27 ports belonging to this dep., in 1841, amounted to <math>23,597</math> vessels = <math>709,038</math> tons, of which 107,291 tons were employed in foreign trade, and 601,747 in the coasting-trade.—The administrative and judicial division of this dep. is into 6 arrondissements, 40 cantons, and 481 communes. The CHA The average pop. per square kilometre is 71.50; that for all France being 67.088. The number of electors under the late regime was 3,624; number of deputies, 7.—This dep. forms the diocese of the bishop of La Rochelle. In 1839, it had 4 communal colleges with 553 pupils; 486 primary boys' schools with 14,337 scholars; and 140 primary girls' schools with 3,160 scholars.—The annual territorial revenue is estimated at 22,637,000 francs.
CHARENTENAY, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Coulange-la- Vineuse. Pop. 713.
CHARENTON, a canton, commune, and village Amand-Mont-Rond. The cant. comprises 9 com.

CHARENTON-LE-PONT, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Seine, arrond. of Sceaux.—The cant. comprises 11 com. Pop. in 1831, 14,846.—The com. and town are on the r. bank of the Marne, 4 m. SE of Paris. Pop. 3,393. Two of the detached forts intended to defend Paris guard the passage of the Seine here.
CHARENTON-SAINT-MAURICE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Seine, cant. of Charenton-le-Pont, 5 m. SE of Paris, on the r. bank of the Marne. Pop. 1,449.
CHARETTE, a township in Warren co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. 1,332.
CHARFIELD, a parish in Gloucestershire, 2 m. N of Wickwar. Area 1,480 acres. Pop. 471.
CHARFORD (North), a parish in Hampshire, 33 m. NNE of Fordingbridge. Area 570 acres. Pop. 54.
CHARFORD (South), a parish adjoining the above. Area 280 acres. Pop. 62.
CHARGE' (EL). See KHARJEH.

CHARIKAR, a town in the Kohistan of Cabul, between Charkal and Tutam-dara, at the mouth of the Ghirbund valley. It contains about 1,000 houses, and carries on an active trade with the districts on either side of the Hindu-kush. It is the residence of the hakim or governor of the Kohistan.
CHARING, a parish in Kent, 13½ m. ESE of Maidstone. Area 4,060 acres. Pop. 1,241.

CHARITE' (LA), a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Nievre.-The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. in 1831, 13,963.—The com. and town are situated on the r. bank of the Loire, 16 m. S of Cosne, at an alt. of 162 metres or 531 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 5,052. It has an extensive trade in iron, corn, fire-wood, and charcoal.

CHARITON, a county in the state of Missouri, U. S., on a river of the same name. Area 832 sq. m. The cap. is Keytesville. Pop. 4,746.—Also a village in St. Mary's p. in the state of Louisiana, 153 m. W by S of New Orleans.—Also a township in Howard co. in Missouri. Pop. 3,113.—Also a village in the county and at the mouth of the river of the same name in Missouri.—Also a township in Macon co. in the state of Missouri. Pop. 806.
CHARITON, a river of Missouri, U. S., which rises in Iowa, and after a S course of 130 m. falls into the Missouri, in the S part of Chariton co. It is navigable by boats for 50 m.
CHARJUI, a town of Bokhara, near the Oxus, 65 m. SSW of Bokhara, on the road from Betik マクト・ドゥトゥトゥクゥクゥクゥクゥクゥクゥクゥクゥクゥカット・メント・メント・メント・メント・メント・メント・メント・メント・メント・メン
CHARKOV. See KHARKOV.
CHARLBURY, a parish and town in Oxfordshire, 4 m. SSE of Chipping-Norton. Area of p. 11,320 acres. Pop. 2,982.
CHARLCOMBE, a parish in Somersetshire, 2 m. N of Bath. Area 570 acres. Pop. 84.
CHARLCOTE, a parish in Warwickshire, 6 m. NW of Kington. Area 2,190 acres. Pop. 267.

CHARLEMONT, a parish and market-town in co. Armagh. Area of p. 3,156 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,489.—The town is situated on the r. bank of the Blackwater, 43 m. SE by S of Dungannon, opposite to the town of Moy. On an eminence a little below the bridge which unites the two towns is the castle of C., a place of some strength. Pop. of the town, exclusive of the garrison, 485.

CHARLEMONT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, com. of Givet, on a commanding eminence on the l. bank of the Meuse. A very strong fortress was founded here by Charles V. in 1555. It was enlarged and strengthened by Vauban; and is considered one of the strongest fortresses on the continent of Europe.
CHARLEMONT, a township in Franklin co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 109 m. W by N of Boston. Pop. 1,127.

CHARLEROI, a town of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, on the Sambre, 20 m. E of Mons. Pop. 7,512. It was anciently a village called Charnoy; but received the name of Charleroi from Charles II. on his founding a strong citadel here in 1666. The town is a place of active trade; and possesses extensive nail-manufactories, forges, and iron-foundries. The railway from Braine-le-Comte to Namur has a station at C.; and a railway from C. to Erquelinnes is now in progress, which will shorten the distance from Paris to Brussels nearly 30 m. The C. canal, by joining that of Brussels, unites the Sambre and the Meuse with the Rupel and Scheldt. Coal-mines and slate-quarries are wrought in the neighbourhood.
CHARLES, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. NNW of South Molton. Area 710 acres. Pop. 362.
CHARLES, a county in the SW of the state of Maryland, U. S., on the Potomac river. Area 450 sq. m. Pop. 16,023. The cap. is Port Tobacco, 72 m. SW of Annapolis.

CHARLES, an island in Hudson's strait, in N lat. 62° 40′, W long. 75°.—Also an island on the N coast of the gulf of St. Lawrence, in N lat. 50° 55'. It is 3 m. in length, and about 1 m. wide; and rises to an alt. of about 200 ft. On its N side is a narrow but secure harbour.—Also an island on the SE coast of Van Diemen's Land, one of the Actaon group.
CHARLES (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of Virginia, U. S., forming the N side of the entrance into Chesapeake bay, in N lat. 37° 3', W long. 76° 2'.

CHARLES (CAPE SAINT), the SE point of Labrador, at the N entrance of Belleisle strait. On the E side of the cape is a harbour formed by three small islands, one of which, 1 m. SE by E of the cape, called St. C. island, is in 52° 12' N lat., 55° 34' W long. There is a clear and deep channel between these islands and the cape.—On the main, a river called the St. C. river discharges itself by a mouth nearly 1 m. in breadth; but at about 2 m. from the sea its channel becomes narrow and intricate, though it is navigable for 3\frac{1}{2} m.
CHARLES CITY, a county in the SE of the state of Virginia, U. S., on the S of the Chickahominy river. Area 208 sq. m. Pop. 4,774.—Its cap., of the same name, is 45 m. SE by E of Richmond.

CHARLES (SAINT), a parish in the SE part of the state of Louisiana, U. S., intersected by the Mississippi. Area 512 sq. m. Pop. 4,700. — Also a county in the E part of Missouri, between the Mis- | are in the city 20 churches, of which the Episcopa- 在大学中，我们的一个女子，我们的一个女子，我们的一个女子，我们就是这个女子，我们就是一个女子，我们就是一个女子，我们就是一个女子，我们的一个女子，我们就会会会 CHA sissippi and the Missouri. Area 470 sq. m. Pop. 7,911. Its cap., of the same name, is 20 m. NW of St. Louis, on the N bank of the Missouri. Pop. 2,818.
CHARLES (SAINT), a river of Lower Canada, rising in a small lake of the same name, 4 m. in length, and flowing into the Saint Lawrence near Quebec, after a course of 15 m.

CHARLES'S ISLAND, or FLORIANA, one of the Galapagos islands, in S lat. 1° 17', W long. 90° 30'. It is about 20 m. in length from N to S, and 15 m. in breadth. Its formation is entirely volcanic. It was colonized in 1832; and, from its neighbourhood to what is called the off-shore whaling-ground by the American whalers, will probably attain some importance.

CHARLESTON, a city and seaport of S Carolina, the capital of a district of the same name, the largest city in the Atlantic states S of the Potomac, and the ninth in population in the United States, situated on a tongue of land formed by the junction of Ashley and Cooper rivers, in 32° 46′ 33" N lat., and 79° 57' 27" W long. from Greenwich; and 3° W long. from Washington. It is 124 m. SSE from Columbia; 118 m. NE from Savannah; 590 m. SSW from Baltimore; 780 m. SSW from New York; and 540 m. SSW from Washington. The bay formed at the junction of Ashley and Cooper rivers is 2 m. wide, and extends S by E 7 m. to its entrance into the Atlantic below Sullivan's island, 7 m. SE of the city. Ashley river is 2,100 yards wide opposite the town; and Cooper river, 1,400; and both are deep, and navigable for large vessels. A sand-bar extends across the mouth of the harbour, but has four entrances, the deepest of which, passing near Sullivan's island, has 17 ft. of water at high tide. It is defended by Fort Moultrie, which—though then comparatively a weak fortress— repulsed a powerful attack of the British fleet under Sir Peter Parker, June 28th, 1776. It is further defended by Fort Pinkney, on an island 2 m. below the city, and by Fort Johnson, 4 m. below. The harbour is open to E winds; and storms from that quarter are often troublesome to shipping at the wharves. The ground on which Charleston is built is only raised about 7 ft. above high tide, so that parts of the city have been overflowed when wind and tide have combined to effect a high flood; but this has not often occurred. The main streets of C., which are from 35 to 70 ft. in width, extend from E to W from the Cooper to the Ashley river; and are intersected by others, at nearly right angles, running from N to S. Many of the houses are of brick, others are of wood, and some of them painted white, which, with the profusion of foliage by which they are commonly surrounded, gives them a smart appearance. The houses are generally elegant; and they are often furnished with piazzas which extend to the roof, and are ornamented with vines. The gardens are adorned with orange, peach, and other trees, and a variety of shrubbery; while the streets are in some quarters lined with 'the Pride of India,' and other trees. Among the public buildings are the city-hall, the exchange, a court-house, jail, 2 arsenals, a theatre, 7 banks, 2 college-halls, an alms-house, and an orphan asylum accommodating 250 destitute children. The Literary and Philosophical society of C. has a fine collection of objects in natural history; and the Academy of fine arts possesses some valuable paintings. The city-library contains about 18,000 volumes .- The college of C., founded in 1795, has in its scientific department about 60 students, and a library of 3,000 vols. It has a president and 4 professors. There is also a Medical college, founded in 1833, with 8 professors, and about 150 students. There lians have 4, the Presbyterians 3, the Methodists 3, the Congregationalists 2, the Roman Catholics 2, and various others. The pop. in 1790 was 16,359; in 1800, 18,711; in 1810, 24,711; in 1820, 24,780; in 1830, 30,289; in 1840, 29,261; of whom 14,673 were slaves. In the last-mentioned year there were reckoned of the pop. as employed in commerce, 676; in manufactures and trades, 1,025; in navigating the ocean, 292; in learned professions, 226. The number of academies and grammar-schools was 14, with 862 students. There were 13 common and primary schools, with 574 scholars, of which 567 were at the public charge. Refinement and hospitality are said to characterise the society of C. The city is considered more healthy during the summer months than the surrounding country. St. Philip's parish, or 'the Neck,' virtually a part of the city, though beyond its chartered limits, contained in 1841 a pop. of 11,876. It is adorned with plantations in a high state of cultivation. Moultrieville, on Sullivan's island, at the mouth of the harbour, is a small but pleasant town; and the refreshing breezes from the ocean cause it to be much resorted to from the city during the summer and autumnal months. The commerce of C. is extensive, comprising that of nearly the whole of the state. Its tonnage in 1840 was 29,250 tons.—There were in 1840, 27 foreign commercial and 34 commission-houses, with a cap. of 3,563,750 dollars; 428 retail stores, cap. 3,317,450 d.; 7 lumber yards, cap. 50,000 d.; 3 grist-mills, 4 saw-mills, with a total cap. of 334,000 d.; 8 printingoffices, 5 binderies, 3 daily, 3 weekly, and 2 semiweekly newspapers, and 4 periodicals, with a cap. of 120,000 d.; 84 brick and stone houses, and 26 wooden, built at the cost of 927,000 d. Total capital in manufactures, 770,500 d.—Three lines of packet-ships ply between this city and New York. One line has 6 ships, one of which sails from each place every 5 days. Another consists of 8 brigs, one of which sails every 4th day. There is another line consisting of 6 brigs.—A canal of 22 m. in length connects the harbour with the Santee river.—A railroad extends 136 m. to Hamburg, on the Savannah.—The district of C. contains 2,244 sq. m. The Santee river forms its NE boundary, and it is watered by Ashley and Cooper rivers. Pop. in 1830, 86,338; in 1840, 82,661. Of the latter pop. 58,539 were slaves, and 3,201 free coloured. C. was first settled in 1680. In 1690, a colony of French refugees, exiled in consequence of the revocation of the edict of Nantz, settled in Carolina, and some of them in C., from whom some of the present respectable inhabitants are descended. At the close of 1779, the city was captured by the British, who had possession of it until the middle of the following May. In 1778, a fire consumed 252 houses; another, in 1796, destroyed about one-third of the city, and occasioned a loss of 2,500,000 d.

CHARLESTON, a township in Penobscot co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 98 m. NE of Augusta. Pop. 1,269.—Also a township in Montgomery co., in the state of New York, 40 m. WNW of Albany. Pop. 2,103.—Also a township in Tioga co. Pennsylvania, 146 m. N of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,010.—Also a township in Kalamazoo co., Michigan, 127 m. W of Detroit. Pop. 605.—Also a township in Orleans co., in Vermont, 55 m. NE of Montpelier. Pop. 731.—Also a v. in Tallahatchee co., Mississippi, 136 m. N of Jackson.

CHARLESTOWN, a village in the p. of Kilbride- Langan, King's co., in the vale of the Brosna, near the town of Clara. Pop. 202.—Also a p. in co. Louth, 24 m. NNW of Ardee. Area 2,699 acres. Pop. 1,390.—Also a v. in the p. of Montiaghs, co. Armagh. Pop. 122.

CHARLESTOWN, a village in the p. of Dun- CHA fermline, Fifeshire, 31 m. S of Dunfermline, on the frith of Forth. It has a small harbour, and exports coal and lime to a large amount. Pop. in 1831, 891; in 1841, 772.—Also a village in the p. of Aboyne, in Aberdeenshire, 26 m. E of Castleton-of-Braemar.

CHARLESTOWN, a small port in the p. of St. Ausbell, Cornwall. Large shipments are made from this place of an artificial kind of clay prepared from granite, which is used by the china manufacturers of Worcestershire.

CHARLESTOWN, a township in Sullivan co., in the state of New Hampshire, U.S., 54 m. W of Concord, on the E side of Connecticut river. Pop. 1,722. This town is connected with Springfield, in the state of Vermont, by a bridge crossing the Connecticut river.-Also a township in Middlesex co., in Massachusetts, 1 m. N of Boston, on the peninsula between Charles and Mystic rivers; connected with Boston by two bridges; and with Chelsea and Malden by two bridges crossing the Mystic. The navy-yard in this township covers 60 acres, and contains two large covered slips, under which vessels of the line are built. Breed's hill, commonly called Bunker's hill, celebrated as the scene of the engagement between the Americans and the British troops on June 17th, 1775, is immediately in the rear of this place. Pop. 1,184.— Also a township in Washington co., Rhode island, 45 m. SSW of Providence, watered by Charles river, and a branch of the Pawcatuck. Pop. 923.—Also a township in Portage co., in Ohio, 145 m. NE of Columbus. Pop. 851.—Also a township in Chester co., in Pennsylvania, 82 m. E of Harrisburg. Pop. 1,039.
CHARLESWORTH, a chapelry in the p. of Glossop, in Derbyshire, 8½ m. NNW of Chapel-en-le- Frith. Pop. 1,732.
CHARLETON, a parish in Devonshire, 2 m. SSE of Kingsbridge. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 703.
CHARLETON (QUEEN), a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. NNE of Pensford. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 190.
CHARLEVAL, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Eure, cant. of Ecours, on the Andelle, 9 m. NNW of Les Andelys. Pop. 1,183. There are manufactories of fine woollens and muslins here.

CHARLEVILLE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Ardennes, arrond. of Mezieres.—The cant., comprising 11 com., had a pop. o 15,571 in 1831.—The com. and town are on the l. bank of the Meuse, 1 m. N of Mezieres. It is a place of considerable trade, and manufactures largely nails, fire-arms, hardware, and machinery. It is the seat of a communal college, and has a public library of 24,000 vols. Pop. in 1841, 9,975.
CHARLEVILLE, a parish and town in co. Cork. Area of p. 3,318 acres. Pop. 5,178.—The town is 43 m. SW of Kilmallock, on a streamlet called the Glynn. It has a considerable local trade. Pop. 4,287.

CHARLIEU, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Loire, arrond. of Roanne.—The cant. comprises 14 com. Pop. 14,154. The town is on the r. bank of the Ornin, 10 m. NNE of Roanne. Pop. 3,689. It has manufactories of calicoes and silks, and some tanneries.
CHARLINCH, a parish in Somersetshire, 45 m. W of Bridgewater. Area 1,900 acres. Pop. 215.

CHARLOTTE, a county in the S part of Virginia, U. S., watered by the Little Roanoke river. Area 600 sq. m. Pop. 14,595. The cap., of the same name, is 98 m. WSW of Richmond. Pop. 475 .- Also a township in Washington co., in the state of Maine, 186 m. ENE of Augusta. Pop. 666. - Also a township in Chittenden co., in the state of Vermont, on Lake Champlain. Pop. 1,620.—Also a v. in Monroe co., in the state of New York, at the mouth of the Genesee river.—Also the cap. of Mecklenburg | co., in the state of North Carolina, 158 m. SW of Raleigh, on the E side of Sugar creek. Pop. 1,000. There are rich gold-mines a little to the E of this place.—Also a township in Chautauque co., in the state of New York, on Cassadaga creek. Pop. 1,428.

CHARLOTTE, a county of New Brunswick, lying on the bay of Fundy, and Passamaquoddy bay, and separated from the United States on the W by the St. Croix. It is generally fertile; and its coasts present numerous and safe accessible harbours and creeks. Its cap. is St. Andrew's, on the E side of the mouth of the St. Croix, nearly opposite to Robinstown on the American side.
CHARLOTTE (CAPE), a low cape on the Arctic coast, in N lat. 71° 20', W long. 94° 40'.

CHARLOTTENBRUNN, a town of Prussia, in Silesia, regency and 16 m. SW of Breslaw, circle and 5 m. SE of Waldenburg. Pop. 577. It contains a Lutheran church, and has several annual fairs. In the environs are mineral springs and mines of coal.

CHARLOTTENBURG, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, regency of Potsdam and circle of Teltow-Storkow, on the l. bank of the Spree, 4 m. W of Berlin. Pop. 6,500. Its streets are straight, and some of them are broad and adorned with lines of trees. It contains two churches, barracks, mineral baths, and a fine castle built by Frederick II. and enclosing the mausoleum of Queen Louisa. The environs are studded with villas. C. possesses an extensive porcelain manufactory, a cotton spinningmill, and bleacheries.
CHARLOTTENLUND, a village of Denmark, in the island of Sieland, 5 m. N of Copenhagen. It contains a royal castle built by Christian VI. in 1733.
